title: Evidence-Based Metabolic Health, Obesity, and Diabetes Risk Module
context: Guideline-Aligned Scientific Knowledge for Weight Management and Metabolic Risk in Indian and South Asian Adults
authoritative_sources: World Health Organization (WHO) Obesity and Physical Activity Guidelines
American Diabetes Association (ADA) Standards of Care
Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) Guidelines for Diabetes

sections.section_title: Global and Indian Burden of Obesity and Diabetes

content.global_context: Obesity is a major modifiable risk factor for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and cardiovascular disease.
content.india_specific: India has one of the largest populations of individuals with diabetes globally.
south_asian_phenotype: Higher visceral adiposity at lower BMI
content.public_health_concern: Urbanization, sedentary occupations, high refined carbohydrate intake, and reduced physical activity contribute significantly.

mechanism: Visceral adipose tissue releases inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6).
Chronic inflammation impairs insulin signaling pathways.
Free fatty acids increase hepatic glucose production.
Pancreatic beta-cell stress leads to progressive dysfunction.

obesity_effect: Chronic stress increases cortisol, promoting abdominal fat storage.

sections.section_title: Clinical Classification of Obesity in Asian Populations

content.clinical_guidance: Central obesity assessment is mandatory in South Asian populations.

sections.section_title: Caloric Restriction and Evidence-Based Weight Reduction

content.recommended_deficit: 500–750 kcal/day deficit for moderate weight loss.
content.expected_loss: 0.5–1 kg per week depending on adherence.

metabolic_benefits: Increases GLUT4 transporter activity in muscles
content.resistance_training: Preserves lean mass and improves insulin sensitivity.

recommended_modifications: Replace polished rice with millets or brown rice.

sections.section_title: Limitations of BMI as a Sole Indicator

content.early_type2: Significant weight loss (>10%) may induce remission in early Type 2 diabetes under medical supervision.
content.important_note: Remission does not equal cure; long-term monitoring required.
